title: How Many Industries Can They Keep Shutting Down?
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Political and economic claims remain attributed to the letter and its quoted sources. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The Tamil below is a source-checked reading copy. The opening salutation omitted by OCR, the visibly printed `7 ஆயிரம்`, the page-break sentence obscured by the `ர "9 ரி` header hallucination, mechanical word joins, and malformed punctuation have been repaired from pages 93-98. The frozen source file remains unchanged and authoritative. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: Nokia's mobile-phone manufacturing plant at Sriperumbudur near Chennai has announced that it will close from 1 November. Though this news has shocked the workers and the opposition parties, has the party ruling Tamil Nadu even taken notice of it? Regretfully, one must say that it has not.

The Nokia plant was established at Sriperumbudur near Chennai in 2005 as the world's largest mobile-phone manufacturing facility. When younger brother Dayanidhi Maran was the Union Minister in charge of this sector, he made strenuous efforts and, with the cooperation of the Tamil Nadu Government, brought this factory to Tamil Nadu. Nokia met sixty per cent of the needs of India's 93 crore mobile-phone users. The factory had the capacity to manufacture one and a half crore mobile phones every month and provided employment to young people between eighteen and thirty years of age. In particular, more than sixty per cent of those employed were young women. Last April, Microsoft, the world's leading computer-software company, bought this profitably operating factory. Microsoft also bought Nokia's operations in India and other countries.

The Nokia factory had 8,500 permanent workers, 6,000 trainees and 6,000 contract workers. Another 7,000 people worked at Foxconn, an associated factory. A few months ago, the company quietly forced 6,000 of them out through voluntary retirement. Now an announcement has come that the factory will be closed permanently, costing even those who continued working there their jobs.
In its statement on the closure, the company said:

“Microsoft purchased Nokia's mobile-phone manufacturing business last April. The Sriperumbudur factory is one of its plants. Microsoft, however, has not placed orders for mobile phones. With no orders, the Sriperumbudur plant will close from 1 November. Microsoft has announced that its agreement with Nokia to purchase mobile phones will be cancelled from 1 November, and the decision has therefore been taken to close the plant. The company's stakeholders and the Labour Commissioner have been informed.”

When one company is sold to or acquired by another, approval under Indian company law is required. A company cannot be sold or purchased without clearance from the Company Law Board and no-objection certificates from the Union and State Governments.

In January 2013, the Indian Income Tax Department conducted a search at the Nokia factory. It issued a summons requiring Nokia's Chennai unit to pay Rs. 21,000 crore in outstanding tax and also allowed the amount to be paid in instalments. Nokia paid Rs. 700 crore as its first instalment in June 2013.

In September 2013, Nokia announced that it would sell its factories, services and all its assets in India to Microsoft for US$7.7 billion, equivalent to Rs. 47,000 crore. The Income Tax Department therefore froze the assets and accounts of Nokia's Chennai factory until the tax was paid. Nokia challenged this in the Delhi High Court. The Court ordered only Nokia's accounts to be released, not its assets. In December 2013, the Delhi High Court also released the assets and permitted their sale to Microsoft, subject to certain conditions. Nokia approached the Supreme Court against those conditions in February 2014. In March 2014, the Income Tax Department also approached the Supreme Court seeking to prevent Nokia's sale to Microsoft.

The Tamil Nadu Commercial Taxes Department had also issued Nokia a notice demanding Rs. 2,400 crore in sales tax. These legal complications stalled the agreement to sell the Chennai Nokia unit. Once the agreement with Microsoft became blocked, Nokia gradually reduced mobile-phone production. It then announced a voluntary-retirement scheme for permanent employees last April and pressured the workers to accept it. Seven thousand employees accepted it; 1,600 refused. On 25 April 2014, Microsoft abandoned its decision to acquire Nokia's factory. Nokia then announced that it would close the factory from November. The Nokia workers' protests produced no result. Under the guise of a suspension of production, the company is now trying to close the plant and send home the remaining 1,100 workers.

It is not Nokia alone. Because many companies have continued to close in Tamil Nadu over the past three years, nearly one lakh people have lost their jobs. Power shortages, the Government's misguided approach, its lack of concern for industry, poor labour relations and similar official neglect have made factory closures and job losses a continuing feature of this regime.
Even the ruling party's supporting daily, *Dina Thanthi*, wrote in its editorial today, 11 October 2014:

“At a time when the Union and State Governments are taking steps to establish new factories, the closure of some existing factories has caused grave concern. The Nokia mobile-phone factory was a source of pride for Chennai. This factory at Sriperumbudur is a foreign-owned company. It began production in 2006. The mobile phones manufactured there were in great demand at home and abroad. The Union and State Governments must act immediately to prevent factories from closing. It is deeply distressing that the closure of such factories causes so many workers to lose their jobs.”
Yes, it is *Dina Thanthi* that has written this.
Nokia's workers have poured out their anguish in interviews. It is said that the companies dependent on this factory may also close and that their employees too will be affected.

The Communist Party of India (Marxist) has condemned the closure, and its State Secretary, Comrade Ramakrishnan, has issued a statement. Comrade Soundararajan, honorary president of the Nokia General Workers' Union and a Member of the Legislative Assembly, has said that they will go to court against Nokia management's unlawful closure announcement. He has pointed out that closing the factory requires permission from the State Government, and that the management is using the words “suspension of production” to escape that requirement.

For a long time there have been differences of opinion between the Marxist party and the Labour Progressive Federation over the welfare of Nokia workers. Even so, both unions have been fighting in their own ways to prevent the factory from closing and to protect the welfare of its workers.

To press the Union and State Governments to reopen the Nokia factory, the district Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam will hold a protest at Sriperumbudur at 10 a.m. on Friday, 17 October 2014, under the leadership of Labour Progressive Federation Secretary M. Shanmugam.

But there is supposedly a Government functioning in Tamil Nadu. What action has it taken so far? When this factory was established, the company received concessions from the Union and State Governments. Should it not consult those Governments when shutting the plant? Should the Government not learn what problem the company claims prevents the factory from continuing to operate? Why should this highly profitable factory be closed? Considering the future of its workers, could the Tamil Nadu Government not acquire the factory and continue to run it? Has the Minister concerned taken any action on any of this?

One worker attempted suicide at the factory gate in protest against the closure and was, fortunately, saved. On behalf of the Labour Progressive Federation, letters about the Nokia factory were sent to the Union Finance Minister on 6 June 2014 and to the Union Minister of State for Commerce, Industry and Finance on 1 July 2014. *The Economic Times* also reported that Union Ministers were taking action.

People who once bought domestically manufactured Nokia phones at lower prices will now be forced to buy imported phones. It is the AIADMK Government that has created this situation. This issue has been discussed for the past two years. Should the AIADMK Government not have called the management for talks, ensured that the factory continued operating and protected the jobs of its workers? Were those workers not young people from Tamil Nadu? Does this Government have no duty to protect them? The country now waits to see whether the Tamil Nadu Government will at least awaken and act to protect the workers of the Nokia plant.
With affection,
M.K.
12 October 2014
